title: Command Pattern
description: Encapsulate a request as an object, enabling undo, queuing, logging, and transactional operations

basic.title: Core Intuition
basic.description: Why the Command pattern exists and the fundamental problem it solves

concepts.title: The Crux — Wrap a Request as an Object
concepts.explanation:
The Command pattern encapsulates a request (an action + its parameters + its target) as a standalone object. Instead of directly calling receiver.doSomething(), you create a Command object that knows WHO to call, WHAT to do, and HOW to undo it. This decouples the sender of the request from the object that performs it.

Think about it: without Command, if a button directly calls light.on(), that button is forever married to that light. With Command, the button just calls command.execute() — and you can swap what that command does at any time.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: A restaurant order slip. You tell the waiter what you want, but the waiter doesn't cook — he writes it on a slip and clips it to the kitchen board. That slip IS the command: it captures WHAT to make, can be QUEUED (10 slips on the board), can be UNDONE (cancel the soup!), and can be LOGGED (end-of-night receipts). The waiter (invoker) never needs to know HOW to cook.
keyPoints: A command object encapsulates: the receiver, the action to perform, and the state needed to undo it
The invoker (e.g., remote control, scheduler) doesn't know what the command does — it just calls execute()
The receiver (e.g., light, stereo, database) doesn't know it's being called through a command
This decoupling enables: undo/redo, queuing, logging, scheduling, and transactions

concepts.title: The Four Players
concepts.explanation:
Every Command pattern implementation has exactly four roles:

1. Command (Interface): Declares execute() and undo(). This is the contract.
2. ConcreteCommand: Implements the interface. Holds a reference to the receiver AND saves previous state for undo.
3. Invoker: Stores commands and decides WHEN to execute them. Contains the data structure (Stack for undo, Queue for scheduling, etc.).
4. Receiver: The object that actually does the work. Knows nothing about commands.

The Client creates commands, binds them to receivers, and hands them to the invoker.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: A movie set: The Director (Client) decides what should happen. 'Action!' and 'Cut!' are Commands. The Assistant Director (Invoker) schedules and sequences them. The Camera Crew, Actors, and Lighting Team (Receivers) actually do the work. The commands can be queued in a shot list, reordered, and even undone ('Let's redo that take').
keyPoints: Command interface: execute() + undo() — the universal contract
ConcreteCommand: binds receiver + action + saved state for undo
Invoker: stores commands in a data structure, triggers execute/undo
Receiver: does the actual work, knows nothing about the pattern
Client: creates and configures everything, wires it together

concepts.title: Brain Triggers — When to Think Command
concepts.explanation:
In an interview, these keywords should trigger the Command pattern:

• 'undo/redo' — THE signature trigger. If a system needs undo, think Command immediately.
• 'history of actions' — logging every operation as an object.
• 'queue requests' — store commands for later execution.
• 'schedule tasks' — deferred execution with time-based triggers.
• 'macro/batch' — a composite command holding multiple child commands.
• 'transaction/rollback' — execute a sequence, undo all if one fails.
• 'replay' — re-execute stored commands to rebuild state.
• 'decouple sender from receiver' — the fundamental intent.
• 'button/toolbar/menu actions' — each UI action maps to a command object.
keyPoints: Undo/redo is the #1 brain trigger for Command pattern
Queuing, scheduling, and logging are secondary triggers
Transaction/rollback = Command + Stack + try/catch
If the problem says 'macro' or 'batch operations', think Composite + Command

cppCode.title: Smart Home Remote — Basic Command Pattern in C++
cppCode.explanation: The C++ implementation uses virtual dispatch for polymorphism, shared_ptr for shared ownership of commands in the history stack, and references to receivers (the command doesn't own the light — it just references it). The virtual destructor in the Command base class is essential for proper cleanup through base pointers. Notice how the RemoteControl (invoker) has zero knowledge of Light — it only knows the Command interface.

javaCode.title: Smart Home Remote — Basic Command Pattern in Java
javaCode.explanation: The Java implementation uses interfaces for the Command contract and ArrayDeque as the undo stack. Java's garbage collector handles memory management, so we don't need smart pointers like C++. The pattern is identical in structure — the difference is in language idioms (interface vs abstract class, GC vs smart pointers). The key insight: history.push(cmd) is the magic line that enables undo.

comparison.cpp: Abstract class with virtual methods + virtual destructor
comparison.java: interface keyword with default methods possible

comparison.cpp: std::shared_ptr for shared ownership in history stack
comparison.java: GC handles everything — just store references
Object reference — GC keeps receiver alive as long as command exists

practiceQuestions.title: Text Editor Commands
practiceQuestions.description: Implement a simple text editor with Command pattern. Support three operations: Type (append text), Delete (remove last N characters), and Undo. Each command should be undoable. The editor maintains a string buffer.
hints: TypeCommand saves the text it added, undo removes that exact text
DeleteCommand saves the deleted characters, undo re-appends them
Use a Stack to store executed commands for undo

practiceQuestions.title: Smart Home with Undo/Redo
practiceQuestions.description: Extend the basic smart home remote control to support both undo AND redo. Rules: Execute pushes to undo stack and clears redo stack. Undo pops from undo stack, calls undo(), and pushes to redo stack. Redo pops from redo stack, calls execute(), and pushes to undo stack.
hints: You need TWO stacks: undoStack and redoStack
A new execute() must clear the redo stack (can't redo after a new action)
Redo simply calls execute() again on the popped command

intermediate.title: SOLID Connections & Real-World Usage
intermediate.description: How Command embodies SOLID principles, pairs with other patterns, and maps to real LLD interview problems

concepts.title: SOLID Principles in Command Pattern
concepts.explanation:
The Command pattern is a SOLID powerhouse:

• Single Responsibility (SRP): Each ConcreteCommand has ONE job — encapsulate one specific action. LightOnCommand only turns on lights, nothing else.

• Open/Closed (OCP): Adding redo required ZERO changes to any Command class. We only modified the Invoker. New commands can be added without changing existing code.

• Liskov Substitution (LSP): Any Command can replace any other Command in the invoker. The RemoteControl works with LightOnCommand, StereoPlayCommand, or any future command — they're all substitutable.

• Interface Segregation (ISP): The Command interface is minimal — just execute() and undo(). No bloated interfaces.

• Dependency Inversion (DIP): The Invoker depends on the Command abstraction, not on concrete receivers. High-level modules (RemoteControl) don't depend on low-level modules (Light, Stereo).
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: Think of a universal TV remote. It depends on the CONCEPT of a button press (abstraction), not on any specific TV brand (concrete). You can point it at a Samsung or LG — it doesn't care. That's DIP. And adding a new button (new command) doesn't require rewiring existing buttons — that's OCP.
keyPoints: SRP: Each command class has exactly one responsibility
OCP: New commands added without modifying existing code
LSP: All commands are substitutable through the Command interface
DIP: Invoker depends on Command abstraction, not concrete receivers

concepts.title: Pattern Combinations — Command's Best Friends
concepts.explanation:
Command rarely works alone in real systems:

1. Command + Memento: When undo can't reverse the action (e.g., sending an email), save a full state snapshot (Memento) before executing. Undo restores the snapshot instead of reversing logic.

2. Command + Composite: A MacroCommand is a command that holds a list of child commands. execute() runs all children in order, undo() runs all children's undo() in reverse. Perfect for batch operations.

3. Command + Strategy: When a command needs a configurable algorithm. SortCommand(strategy) lets you swap the sorting algorithm without creating new command classes.

4. Command + Observer: After execute(), notify observers. Dashboard updates, email alerts, and log entries can all subscribe to command completion events.

5. Command + Prototype: Clone a template command for each new request instead of creating from scratch. Useful in task schedulers where the same command template runs multiple times.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: A chef's kitchen: The order slip (Command) might require a specific cooking technique (Strategy). A combo meal order (Composite) contains multiple individual dishes. If the dish can't be uncooked (irreversible), the chef takes a photo first (Memento). And the waiter announces 'Order up!' to all servers (Observer).
keyPoints: Command + Memento = undo for irreversible actions
Command + Composite = macro/batch operations
Command + Strategy = commands with swappable algorithms
Command + Observer = notifications after execution
In interviews, mentioning these combos signals senior-level thinking

concepts.title: Data Structure Pairing — The Right Container for the Job
concepts.explanation:
The command object stays the same — only the CONTAINER changes based on what you need:

• Stack → Undo/Redo (LIFO: last action undone first)
• Queue → Task Processing (FIFO: first submitted, first executed)
• PriorityQueue → Scheduling (sorted by time or priority)
• ArrayList → Audit Log (append-only, never delete)
• List<Command> inside CompositeCommand → Macro/Batch
• Stack + try/catch → Transaction Rollback (undo all on failure)
• Bounded Queue → Rate Limiting (reject when full)
• Persistent Log (DB/Kafka) → Event Sourcing (replay to rebuild state)

The key insight: the data structure in the invoker DETERMINES the capability. Same Command interface, completely different behavior based on the container.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: Think of a sticky note (the command). Put it on a stack of papers → you'll handle the most recent first (undo). Put it in a ticket queue → first come first served. Pin it to a calendar → scheduled execution. File it in a binder → audit trail. The note doesn't change — where you PUT it determines what happens.
keyPoints: Stack = undo/redo (LIFO)
Queue = task processing (FIFO)
PriorityQueue = scheduling by time/priority
ArrayList = audit logging (append-only)
The invoker's data structure defines the system's capability

cppCode.title: Macro Command + Transaction Rollback in C++
cppCode.explanation: This demonstrates two advanced Command capabilities: MacroCommand (Composite pattern) groups multiple commands into a batch, and Transaction Rollback undoes all executed commands in reverse order when any single command fails. The executed_ stack tracks which commands succeeded so that rollback only undoes what was actually done. This is exactly how database transactions work — all or nothing.

javaCode.title: Macro Command + Transaction Rollback in Java
javaCode.explanation: The Java version showcases MacroCommand as a Composite — it holds child commands in a List and tracks executed ones in a Deque. When the second transfer fails (Bob only has $800, can't transfer $1500), the catch block rolls back the first transfer. After rollback, all balances are restored to their original values. This pattern is essential in any LLD problem involving financial transactions, order processing, or multi-step workflows.

comparison.cpp: std::exception with what() method, catch by const reference
std::stack<shared_ptr<Command>> — RAII cleanup on scope exit
comparison.java: Deque<Command> — GC cleanup, manual clear if needed

practiceQuestions.title: Task Scheduler with Priority Queue
practiceQuestions.description: Build a task scheduler that accepts commands with a priority level (1=highest). Commands should execute in priority order, not insertion order. Support: schedule(command, priority), executeNext(), and executeAll(). The scheduler should use a PriorityQueue internally.
hints: Create a wrapper class that holds a Command + priority and implements Comparable
PriorityQueue naturally gives you the highest-priority item first
executeNext() polls from the queue, executeAll() drains it

practiceQuestions.title: Drawing App with Macro Undo
practiceQuestions.description: Build a drawing application that supports: DrawCircle, DrawRectangle, and a MacroCommand that groups multiple draws. The canvas maintains a list of shapes. Each command adds/removes shapes. Implement undo for both individual commands and macro commands (undo should remove all shapes added by the macro in one step).
hints: Canvas receiver holds a List<String> of shape names
DrawCommand saves the index where it inserted the shape for undo
MacroCommand holds List<Command> and undoes all children in reverse order

advanced.title: Expert Patterns & Interview Mastery
advanced.description: Anti-patterns, complex implementations, event sourcing, and interview-ready problem solving

concepts.title: Anti-Patterns — The Traps That Break Your Design
concepts.explanation:
Four traps that catch even experienced developers:

1. God Command: The command contains ALL business logic instead of delegating to a receiver. Symptom: your command class is 200+ lines. Fix: command should be a thin wrapper that calls receiver methods.

2. Forgotten State for Undo: The undo() method can't work because execute() didn't save the previous state. Symptom: undo does nothing or corrupts state. Fix: ALWAYS save relevant state BEFORE executing.

3. Over-Engineering: Using Command when a simple method call would suffice. The YAGNI test: Do you need undo? Queuing? Logging? Scheduling? If ALL answers are NO, don't use Command.

4. Unbounded History: The undo stack grows forever, eating memory. Fix: cap at N commands, drop the oldest. Text editors typically limit to 50-100 undo levels.

5. Partial Execution without Rollback: In a transaction, if step 2 of 3 fails, step 1's effects remain. Fix: use try/catch with rollback — undo all executed commands in reverse order.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: The God Command is like a waiter who cooks, serves, washes dishes, AND manages the restaurant. That's not a waiter — that's a one-person show that will break. The waiter should write the slip and carry it — nothing more.
keyPoints: God Command: delegate to receivers, don't put logic in the command
Forgotten State: always save before execute, restore on undo
Over-Engineering: apply the YAGNI test before using Command
Unbounded History: cap the stack size
Partial Execution: wrap in try/catch with reverse-order rollback

concepts.title: Command vs Strategy vs Observer — The Decisive Differences
concepts.explanation:
These three patterns are confused constantly in interviews:

Command vs Strategy:
- Strategy picks HOW to do something (algorithm selection). Command wraps WHAT to do (request encapsulation).
- Strategy is stateless — swap and forget. Command carries state for undo.
- Strategy answers: 'Which algorithm?' Command answers: 'What should happen later?'
- One sentence: Strategy picks the recipe; Command writes the order slip.

Command vs Observer:
- Command is one-to-one: invoker → specific command → specific receiver.
- Observer is one-to-many: subject → broadcasts to ALL subscribers.
- Command can be deferred/queued. Observer is usually immediate.
- One sentence: Command is a directed letter; Observer is a radio broadcast.

Command vs Memento:
- Command remembers what you DID (the action). Memento remembers what things LOOKED LIKE (the state).
- Command undo reverses the action. Memento undo restores a snapshot.
- Command is cheap (stores action + delta). Memento is expensive (stores full state).
- One sentence: Command is the recipe to cook and uncook; Memento is a photo of the kitchen.
keyPoints: Command vs Strategy: WHAT to do vs HOW to do it
Command vs Observer: directed letter vs radio broadcast
Command vs Memento: action log vs state snapshot
In interviews, use comparison tables to show clarity of thought

concepts.title: Command at Scale — Event Sourcing
concepts.explanation:
Event Sourcing is the Command pattern taken to the architectural level. Instead of storing current state in a database, you store EVERY command (event) that ever happened. To get current state, you replay all events from the beginning.

Why this is powerful:
- Complete audit trail — you know exactly what happened and when
- Time travel — replay events up to any point to see historical state
- Debugging — reproduce any bug by replaying the exact sequence of events
- Scalability — events can be distributed across systems via message queues (Kafka)

This is how banking ledgers work (every transaction is recorded, balance is derived), how Git works (every commit is a command, current code is the result of replaying all commits), and how Redux works (every action is dispatched, state is derived from the action log).

In an LLD interview, mentioning Event Sourcing when discussing Command pattern shows you understand the pattern at production scale.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: A bank ledger. The bank doesn't store 'Alice has $5000.' It stores every deposit and withdrawal ever made. Alice's balance is DERIVED by replaying all her transactions. If there's a dispute, they can trace the exact sequence. That ledger IS a persistent command log.
keyPoints: Event Sourcing = persistent command log as source of truth
Current state is derived by replaying all events
Enables: audit trails, time travel, debugging, distributed systems
Real examples: banking ledgers, Git, Redux, Kafka event streams
Mentioning this in interviews shows production-level thinking

cppCode.title: Event Sourcing with Command Pattern in C++
cppCode.explanation: Event Sourcing treats commands as events that are persisted forever. The EventStore is the invoker — it stores events in an append-only list (never delete!). To get current state, replay ALL events from the beginning. To time-travel, replay up to event N. This is exactly how Git, Redux, and banking systems work. In production, the EventStore would be backed by Kafka or a database — here we use a vector for simplicity.

javaCode.title: Event Sourcing with Command Pattern in Java
javaCode.explanation: The Java Event Sourcing implementation uses an abstract Event class as the command, ArrayList as the persistent store, and streams for elegant replay. The key insight: state is NEVER stored directly — it's always derived by replaying events. This gives you a complete audit trail, time-travel debugging, and the ability to rebuild state at any point in history. In production, replace ArrayList with Kafka or EventStoreDB.

comparison.cpp: Abstract class with virtual destructor, manual timestamp formatting
comparison.java: Abstract class with LocalTime and DateTimeFormatter

comparison.cpp: Range-based for loop with index boundary check
comparison.java: Stream API with .limit(n) for elegant time travel

comparison.cpp: Apache Kafka C++ client, RocksDB, or custom file-based log
comparison.java: Kafka, EventStoreDB, or Axon Framework (mature ecosystem)

practiceQuestions.title: Chess Move System with Full Undo/Redo
practiceQuestions.description: Design a chess move system using Command pattern. Each move captures: piece, from-square, to-square, and captured piece (if any). Implement execute() to move the piece and undo() to reverse it (restoring any captured piece). Support special moves: castling (moves two pieces) should be a MacroCommand. Maintain a move history with undo/redo capability.
hints: MoveCommand stores: piece, fromSquare, toSquare, capturedPiece (nullable)
CastleCommand is a MacroCommand containing two MoveCommands (king + rook)
The undo of a capture must restore the captured piece to its original square
Use two stacks for undo/redo, clear redo stack on new move

practiceQuestions.title: Database Migration with Transaction Rollback
practiceQuestions.description: Design a database migration system. Each migration is a Command with execute() (apply migration) and undo() (rollback). A MigrationRunner executes migrations in order and supports: migrateUp() runs all pending migrations, migrateDown(n) rolls back last N migrations, and getStatus() shows which migrations have been applied. If any migration fails during migrateUp(), all previously applied migrations in that batch must be rolled back.
hints: Each migration command stores a version number and description
MigrationRunner maintains a list of applied migrations (like a history stack)
migrateUp should use the transaction pattern — rollback on failure
migrateDown pops from the applied list and calls undo()

keyDifferences.title: Command Interface
keyDifferences.cpp: Abstract class with pure virtual methods and virtual destructor
keyDifferences.java: interface with execute() and undo() methods

keyDifferences.title: Memory Management for History
keyDifferences.java: Direct references in Deque/List — GC handles cleanup

keyDifferences.title: Receiver Binding
keyDifferences.java: Object reference — GC keeps receiver alive if command references it

keyDifferences.title: Null Object Pattern
keyDifferences.cpp: NoOpCommand concrete class inheriting from Command
keyDifferences.java: Anonymous class, lambda, or Command.NOOP static field

keyDifferences.title: Composite Command
keyDifferences.cpp: std::vector<shared_ptr<Command>> with reverse iterators for undo
keyDifferences.java: List<Command> with Collections.reverse() or reversed stream

bestPractices: Always save relevant previous state in the ConcreteCommand BEFORE executing — this is essential for undo
Keep commands thin — they should delegate to receivers, not contain business logic
Use the Null Object pattern (NoOpCommand) to avoid null checks in the invoker
Cap the undo history size to prevent memory leaks (typically 50-100 levels)
Clear the redo stack when a new command is executed — you can't redo after a new action
For irreversible actions, combine Command with Memento to snapshot full state
Use MacroCommand (Composite) for batch operations — undo reverses all children in reverse order
In transactions, track which commands succeeded so rollback only undoes what was actually done
Choose the right data structure for the invoker: Stack for undo, Queue for scheduling, List for audit
Apply the YAGNI test: only use Command when you need undo, queuing, logging, or scheduling

commonMistakes.mistake: Putting all business logic inside the command (God Command) instead of delegating to a receiver
commonMistakes.correct: Keep the command as a thin wrapper that calls receiver methods. The receiver holds the business logic.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to save previous state before execute(), making undo impossible or broken
commonMistakes.correct: Always capture the relevant state (e.g., previousVolume, capturedPiece) BEFORE calling receiver methods in execute().

commonMistakes.mistake: Using Command pattern when a simple method call would suffice (no undo, queue, or log needed)
commonMistakes.correct: Apply the YAGNI test: Do you need undo? Queuing? Logging? Scheduling? If all answers are no, use a direct method call.

commonMistakes.mistake: Allowing the undo/redo history stack to grow unbounded, causing memory leaks
commonMistakes.correct: Cap the history at a reasonable size (e.g., 50-100 entries). Drop the oldest command when the limit is reached.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not handling partial execution in transaction-style commands — step 1 succeeds, step 2 fails, step 1 is not rolled back
commonMistakes.correct: Use a try/catch block that tracks executed commands in a stack. On failure, pop and undo each executed command in reverse order.

commonMistakes.mistake: Confusing Command with Strategy — using Command to swap algorithms instead of encapsulating requests
commonMistakes.correct: Strategy = HOW to do something (swap algorithms). Command = WHAT to do (encapsulate request for undo/queue/log). Strategy picks the recipe; Command writes the order slip.
